coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) have gained significant attention as a promising theranostic platform, owing to their excellent biocompatibility, tunable fluorescence, and multifunctional properties. In this work, we present the development of highly luminescent CDs synthesized from natural precursors, engineered to serve as a versatile nanoplatform for nitric oxide (NO) delivery. By functionalizing the surface of the CDs with ruthenium nitrosyl (Ru-NO) complexes and folic acid, we created a targeted system (CDs-FAR) that enables precise drug delivery, real-time fluorescence tracking, and light-triggered NO release. The CDs demonstrated exceptional antioxidant activity, with scavenging efficiencies of ∼77 % and ∼89 % in 2,2-Diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) and 2,2′-azino-bis(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid (ABTS) assays, respectively, underscoring their potential for oxidative stress regulation. Furthermore, controlled NO release was achieved under light irradiation (100–300 mW/cm2), highlighting the system's ability to respond to external stimuli for accurate therapeutic action. This multifunctional platform holds great potential for treating disorders related to nitric oxide deficiency and advancing NO-based cancer therapies, offering a novel approach in the fields of precision medicine and nano-theranostics.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of CDs and their functionalization with folic acid and Ru-complex. Below: the mechanism of light triggered release of NO and cell apoptosis.
UV–visible spectrum (a) and PL intensity (b) of the CDs obtained from chitosan and silk fibroin. (c) Excitation dependent PL spectra of CDs in aqueous medium and (d) XRD of CDs.
(a) FTIR spectra of pure CDs and CDs-FAR (b) PL spectra of CDs and CDs-FAR.
(a) XPS survey spectrum of CDs (b) high resolution spectrum of C1s (c) high resolution spectrum of N1s (d) high resolution spectrum of O1s.
(a) XPS survey spectrum of CDs-FAR and (b) high resolution spectrum of Ru3p.
Antioxidant activity of CDs in (a) DPPH assay (b) ABTS assay and (c) process of antioxidant action.

(a) NO flux was seen from the 0.6 mg/mL CDs-FAR nanoparticles distributed in aerobic saline solution when illuminated by visible light pulses of varying intensities: (a) 100 mW/cm2, (b) 200 mW/cm2, and (c) 300 mW/cm2. Light-induced NO release (d) from 0.6 mg/mL CDs-FAR nanoparticles suspended in anaerobic saline solution via continuous light exposure (λ > 400 nm, 300 mW/cm2).

(a) Death of HeLa cells, induced by dark and visible light, following treatment with varying doses of CDs-folic acid and CDs-FAR nanoparticles (0–100 μg/mL) throughout a 6-hour incubation period at 400 nm wavelength, 200 mW/cm2 intensity for 10 min. (b) Detection of singlet oxygen species using a 1O2 trap, CHDDE. Graph of sodium 1,3-cyclohexadiene-1,4-diethanoate (CHDDE) relative concentration against irradiation duration, catalyzed by CDs-folic acid and CDs-FAR nanoparticles.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) have gained significant attention as a promising theranostic platform, owing to their excellent biocompatibility, tunable fluorescence, and multifunctional properties. In this work, we present the development of highly luminescent CDs synthesized from natural precursors, engineered to serve as a versatile nanoplatform for nitric oxide (NO) delivery. By functionalizing the surface of the CDs with ruthenium nitrosyl (Ru-NO) complexes and folic acid, we created a targeted system (CDs-FAR) that enables precise drug delivery, real-time fluorescence tracking, and light-triggered NO release. The CDs demonstrated exceptional antioxidant activity, with scavenging efficiencies of ∼77 % and ∼89 % in 2,2-Diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) and 2,2′-azino-bis(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid (ABTS) assays, respectively, underscoring their potential for oxidative stress regulation. Furthermore, controlled NO release was achieved under light irradiation (100–300 mW/cm2), highlighting the system's ability to respond to external stimuli for accurate therapeutic action. This multifunctional platform holds great potential for treating disorders related to nitric oxide deficiency and advancing NO-based cancer therapies, offering a novel approach in the fields of precision medicine and nano-theranostics.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nitric oxide (NO) plays a significant role in numerous physiological and obsessive practices and holds great promise for therapeutic applications, particularly in anticancer treatments [1]. However, the therapeutic potential of NO is constrained by its short half-life under physiological conditions and its high reactivity with oxygen, superoxide, metalloenzymes, and thiols, which significantly limits its stability and efficacy [2]. The anticancer effects of NO are highly concentration-dependent at the delivery site; micromolar or higher concentrations can induce apoptosis, whereas sub-micromolar levels may facilitate tumor proliferation [3,4]. Therefore, it is imperative to create NO-delivery systems that enable spatiotemporal regulation and precise dose application at the target location to attain the required therapeutic outcomes [5]. A variety of NO donors and delivery systems have been documented for therapeutic applications, with particular emphasis on light-triggered NO release due to the ease of light manipulation for regulating NO release [6–8]. However, most photo-responsive NO-delivery systems require UV or visible light, which suffer from limited tissue penetration and high phototoxicity. Conversely, near-infrared (NIR) light, especially at 808 nm, provides enhanced tissue penetration and less phototoxic effects, rendering it a more appropriate stimulus for in vivo applications than the frequently utilized 980 nm light, which causes considerable water heating [9,10]. While several studies have demonstrated NIR-light-activated NO release, their therapeutic efficacy in vivo, particularly for cancer treatment, remains underexplored [11–13]. Thermosensitive hydrogels have garnered interest for their ability to emit nitric oxide in antibacterial and wound-healing applications. These hydrogels demonstrate remarkable characteristics, such as superior biocompatibility, low cytotoxicity to host cells, and efficient growth to asymmetrical superficial lesions, rendering them particularly effective against infections [14,15].

Among various NO donors, photolabile ruthenium nitrosyls (Ru-NOs) have garnered interest due to their low cytotoxicity, substantial durability in diverse biopharmaceutical environments, and ability to release NO in a controlled manner upon light activation [16,17]. Similar to other ruthenium complexes, ruthenium nitrosyls exhibit substitutional inertness at ambient temperature. Nevertheless, certain nitrosyls emit NO upon exposure to light. Ruthenium nitrosyl complexes, such as K2[Ru(NO)(Cl)5], are particularly promising for their robust photoreactivity and capability to release NO under UV or visible light, offering a tunable and efficient NO delivery mechanism [18]. In ruthenium chemistry, the [Ru–NO]6 structure is typically recognized as NO+ coordinated to a Ru(II) core. This is primarily attributed to the elevated NO stretching frequencies (νNO) 1960–1820 cm−1 seen in [Ru–NO]6 nitrosyls, in contrast to free NO (∼1750 cm−1) or bound NO radical (1750–1650 cm−1) in [Ru–NO]7 species [19]. Mitochondria, the cellular powerhouse, are widely acknowledged as potential targets for cancer treatment. Increased nitric oxide levels, especially those generated by inducible nitric oxide synthase (iNOS), might impede mitochondrial respiration, therefore affecting cellular survival [20]. Carbon dot-based nitric oxide delivery nanoplatforms, functionalized with S-nitrosothiol or nitrobenzene derivatives and incorporating a triphenylphosphonium (TPP) moiety, have demonstrated the capability to precisely target the mitochondrial organelle, facilitating NO delivery upon visible light irradiation [21,22].

Carbon dots (CDs), nanomaterials with dimensions below 10 nm, are emerging as versatile nanomaterials characterized by their zero-dimensional, quasi-spherical structure [23,24]. Among their numerous variants, N-doped CDs stand out due to their exceptional properties, including diminutive size, vivid blue fluorescence, and high photostability, making them ideal candidates for theranostic applications, particularly in monitoring the intracellular dynamics of drug delivery systems [25–28]. Other nanoparticles, such as nanodiamonds and carbon nanostructures, possess unique properties that make them attractive for biomedical applications such as drug delivery and bioimaging [29]. Apart from other nanostructures, CDs provide enhanced biocompatibility, adjustable surface chemistry, exceptional photostability, and effective nitric oxide release upon light activation. Moreover, their tiny size and aqueous solubility facilitate cellular absorption and precise distribution, rendering them an ideal choice for biomedical applications [30,31]. Structurally, CDs are primarily composed of carbon, with minor contributions from heteroatoms such as oxygen, hydrogen, and nitrogen, depending on the choice of precursor and reaction conditions [32–34]. The sp2-hybridized carbon framework, interspersed with a small fraction of sp3 centers, provides CDs with distinct chemical and electronic properties. These materials exhibit remarkable hydrophilicity, robust fluorescence, exceptional photostability, and minimal cytotoxicity, alongside resistance to photobleaching, in contrast to current carbon-based materials [35,36]. These superior properties render CDs highly attractive for a wide range of applications, including drug delivery, bioimaging, sensing, antioxidant therapies, and photocatalysis, as alternatives to heavy metal-derived semiconductor dots, polymeric dots, and organic dyes [37,38]. Recent reports indicate that nanohybrids of CD-conjugated nitroaniline derivatives exhibit nitric oxide production under light irradiation [39]. Similarly, polyethyleneimine-stabilized CDs, functioning as photoluminescence probes, have been incorporated into carboxymethyl chitosan nanospheres through electrostatic interactions, combined with a photosensitive nitric oxide donor [Fe4S3(NO)7]− (Roussin's black salt), enabling light-triggered NO release while simultaneously allowing fluorescence-based monitoring [40].

This research presents a novel nitric oxide (NO) delivery nanoplatform, [CDs-FAR], created by conjugating carbon dots with ruthenium nitrosyl (Ru-NO) as a NO donor and folic acid (FA), covalently attached to the carbon dot surface, for targeted administration. The [CDs-FAR] nanoplatform enables light-activated, spatiotemporal nitric oxide production and permits real-time fluorescence observation. This technology signifies a significant enhancement in NO-based therapy tactics by integrating precise targeting with regulated NO administration, providing greater precision and efficacy than traditional methods. The aim of this study is to develop a novel nitric oxide (NO) delivery system, [CDs-FAR], by conjugating carbon dots with ruthenium nitrosyl (Ru-NO) complexes as an NO donor and folic acid (FA) for targeted delivery. This system is designed to enable light-triggered, spatiotemporal release of NO while allowing real-time fluorescence tracking. Through precise targeting and controlled NO delivery, this work aims to enhance the precision and efficacy of NO-based therapeutic strategies, overcoming the limitations of traditional approaches. Ultimately, this study highlights the potential of carbon dots as multifunctional carriers for advancing cancer therapy and broadening the application of stimuli-responsive NO delivery systems.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Extraction of silk fibroin from Bombyx mori silk cocoons
ce:section.ce:para: Silk fibroin (SF) was extracted from Bombyx mori silk cocoons using a well-established protocol [41]. The cocoons were divided into smaller fragments and submerged in a 0.02 M Na2CO3 solution for a duration of 1 h to remove sericin. The resulting fibers were undergoing a cleansing process and were desiccated overnight at room temperature. The dried fibers were then dissolved in a lithium bromide solution at 70 °C for 4 h, after which dialysis was performed against deionized water for 72 h using a dialysis membrane with a molecular weight cutoff (MWCO) of 12–14 kDa to remove lithium bromide. The solution underwent centrifugation at 9000 rpm for 20 min at 4 °C to remove impurities. The purified SF solution was desiccated and preserved at 4 °C prior to utilization.

Fluorescent CDs were manufactured via the pyrolysis of a physical mixture of chitosan and silk fibroin in a ratio of 1:1.5. The mixture was carbonized at 200 °C for 4 h in an inert environment. The resultant substance was pulverized into a fine powder and subsequently dissolved in water. The solution was subsequently filtered to exclude insoluble impurities. The filtrate was collected, dialyzed, lyophilized, and kept at 4 °C for future use.

To fabricate the NO-anchored carbon dots (CDs-FAR), 72 mg of ruthenium nitrosyl (Ru-NO) and 4 mg of folic acid (FA) were initially dissolved in 5 mL of N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF). This solution was activated with 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)carbodiimide (EDC) and N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) for 30 min to facilitate conjugation. Subsequent to activation, 35 mg of CDs was incorporated into the mixture and permitted to react for 24 h at ambient temperature. Post-reaction, unreacted components were removed through dialysis (MWCO 1000 Da). The filtered solution within the dialysis bag was subsequently freeze-dried, yielding in the final CDs-FAR nanoparticle. This study describes the efficient synthesis and surface modification of carbon dots by the functionalization of naturally derived carbon dots with folic acid and a ruthenium-nitrosyl complex.

The synthesized CDs-FAR was extensively characterized using advanced analytical techniques. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) confirmed the successful conjugation of Ru-NO and FA to the CDs. UV–visible and Photoluminescence spectroscopy demonstrated the optical properties and stability of the nanoplatform. Structural properties were analyzed using X-ray diffraction (XRD), and functional groups were identified through Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy. Details regarding the materials, reagents, and equipment used in the synthesis and characterization of CDs-FAR, as well as comprehensive descriptions of the analytical techniques employed (e.g., XPS, PL spectroscopy, UV–visible spectroscopy, XRD, and FTIR), are thoroughly presented in the Supporting Information.

Antioxidant properties of the synthesized CDs-FAR nanoplatform were evaluated using standard assays. Detailed methodologies, including DPPH and ABTS radical scavenging assays, are provided in the Supporting Information.

The light-triggered nitric oxide (NO) release behavior of the CDs-FAR was systematically investigated. Experimental conditions, light intensity parameters, and quantification methods are comprehensively described in the Supporting Information.
The complete procedure and calculations for quantum yield determination are documented in the Supporting Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detailed synthesis and characterizations of CDs-FAR
ce:section.ce:para: The fabrication of CDs-FAR entails a multistep functionalization procedure, as depicted in Fig. 1 . The CDs were initially produced to incorporate surface amino groups, which offer reactive sites for further conjugation. To get dual functionality, a ruthenium nitrosyl (Ru-NO) complex, noted for its NO releasing properties, was covalently conjugated with folic acid (FA), which is distinguished by its selective targeting of folate receptors that are overexpressed in many cell types, on the surface of the CDs. The synthesis commenced with the formulation of the Ru-NO complex and FA in DMF, resulting in a solution with exact molar concentrations of each constituent. The mixture was further activated by including a combination of EDC and NHS, which promotes the formation of amide bonds between the carboxyl groups on FA and Ru-NO and the amino groups on the CDs. This activation phase guarantees that the Ru-NO and FA molecules are prepared for effective covalent bonding, hence improving the stability and endurance of the final nanoplatform. For detailed synthesis procedures, refer to the Experimental section.

The linked Ru-NO complex facilitates regulated NO release upon light exposure, while the conjugated folic acid enhances targeted administration by facilitating cellular uptake in environments rich in folate receptors. This multistep synthesis strategy emphasizes the design concept of integrating therapeutic and targeting functions into a single nanoplatform, positioning CDs-FAR as a promising candidate for targeted NO delivery in biomedical applications. Fig. 1 confirms that the synthesized CDs-FAR nanoplatform retains the intrinsic fluorescence properties of the parent CDs, ensuring its utility for real-time monitoring and bioimaging. Therefore, by coupling the NO-releasing capability of Ru-NO with the targeting efficiency of FA, the CDs-FAR nanoplatform represents a significant advancement in the field of nanotheranostics. Fig. 1(below) illustrates the light-triggered NO release mechanism from Ru2+-NO-functionalized carbon dots (CDs-FAR) and its role in inducing mitochondrial bursting. Upon light stimulation, CDs-FAR releases NO, which penetrates the cell and accumulates in the mitochondria. The excessive NO disrupts mitochondrial integrity, leading to mitochondrial bursting and subsequent cell apoptosis. This schematic highlights the controlled NO delivery mechanism and its potential therapeutic applications.

The TEM image (Fig. S1a) shows the morphology of the prepared carbon dots (CDs), revealing their nanoscale size and uniform dispersion. The particle size of the CDs, as determined from the TEM analysis, is ranging from 7.2 to 9.4 nm. The SEM image (Fig. S1b) provides further insight into the surface morphology of the CDs. The presence of agglomerations, highlighted by yellow dotted circles, indicates the high surface energy of the nanoparticles, leading to partial aggregation. This supports the stability and structural characteristics of the synthesized CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical and structural characterization of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2 illustrates the comprehensive optical and structural properties of CDs derived from chitosan and silk fibroin through UV–Vis absorption, photoluminescence (PL) spectra and XRD analysis. Fig. 2(a) displays the UV–visible absorption spectrum primarily exhibits a strong absorption band in the UV region with a prominent peak between 250 and 300 nm, likely resulting from the π-π* transitions of CC bonds and n-π* transitions from CO or nitrogen-containing groups on the surface of the carbon dots [42]. Additionally, functional groups such as carboxyl, hydroxyl, and amine groups contribute supplementary energy states [39], broadening the absorption range to encompass wavelengths between 300 and 450 nm. This absorption profile is indicative of CDs and signifies the existence of aromatic or conjugated structures derived from the precursor components.

Fig. 2(b) illustrates the photoluminescence spectra of the CDs under a singular excitation wavelength of 350 nm, revealing a pronounced PL emission peak at around 450 nm. This emission is attributed to the intrinsic fluorescent nature of the CDs, likely arising from surface defects and quantum confinement effects within the nanoscale structure. Fig. 2(c) further investigates the excitation-dependent photoluminescence behavior, indicating that the emission peak transitions from approximately 430 nm to 490 nm as the excitation wavelength increases from 290 to 380 nm. This excitation-dependent activity is characteristic of carbon dots and may result from the existence of various emissive sites or surface states, along with potential variations in particle size distribution [43–45]. The excitation-dependent tunability enhances the applicability of these CDs for bioimaging and sensing, enabling specific emissions under different stimulation wavelengths.

Fig. 2d presents the XRD pattern of the CDs, characterized by a large diffraction peak located between 20° and 30°, indicative of the amorphous carbon structure often observed in CDs. The comprehensive diffraction pattern reveals a lack of crystalline structure, characteristic of CDs derived from organic precursors such chitosan and silk fibroin [46]. Furthermore, the functionalization process, such as conjugation with Ru-NO and folic acid, does not significantly alter the amorphous structure, as suggested by comparable patterns expected for CDs-FAR (not presented here). The optical and structural analyses collectively validate the successful synthesis of CDs, showcasing their tunable fluorescence and robust amorphous framework, making them highly suitable for biomedical applications, including bioimaging, sensing, and controlled NO delivery.

Fig. S2 presents the Raman spectroscopy analysis of the synthesized carbon dots CDs, confirming their structural characteristics. The spectrum exhibits two prominent peaks: the D-band at 1351 cm−1, associated with disordered carbon or structural defects, and the G-band at 1548 cm−1, corresponding to the graphitic sp2 carbon structure. The presence of both bands indicates a partially graphitized structure with some degree of disorder, which is typical for CDs. The intensity ratio provides insight into the degree of disorder and graphitization, influencing the electronic and optical properties of the CDs. This characterization further supports the successful synthesis of CDs and complements the UV–Vis spectroscopy results by providing direct evidence of their carbon-based composition.

Fig. 3 presents the comparative characterization of pure CDs synthesized from a chitosan and silk fibroin mixture and their functionalized counterpart, CDs-FAR, which incorporates covalently bonded Ru-NO and FA. Fig. 3(a) illustrates the FTIR spectra of CDs and CDs-FAR, showcasing key differences in functional groups between the two. Both spectra feature a prominent broad peak at ∼3290 cm−1, corresponding to OH and NH stretching vibrations, which confirms the presence of hydroxyl and amine groups on the CD surfaces. In CDs-FAR, new peaks emerge at 1875 cm−1, corresponding to the stretching vibrations of Ru-NO, corroborating successful functionalization with Ru-NO [11,47]. The distinctive signal at 1652 cm−1, linked to CO stretching, is present in both samples. This peak presumably originates from the amide bonds intrinsic to chitosan and silk fibroin, suggesting that the functionalization process maintains the structural integrity of the parent CDs.

Fig. 3(b) compares the photoluminescence (PL) spectra of pure CDs and CDs-FAR under equivalent excitation settings (λex = 350 nm). The CDs exhibit a strong emission peak centered at ∼450 nm, attributed to surface states and quantum confinement effects. Upon functionalization, CDs-FAR shows a notable decrease in PL intensity alongside a minor red shift in the emission peak. This quenching effect can be attributed to the conjugation of Ru-NO and FA, which likely introduces non-radiative deactivation pathways or modifies the surface energy states of the CDs. The red shift further suggests changes in the environment due to functionalization process. The change in PL properties of CDs-FAR, characterized by reduced intensity and red-shifted emission, provide a dual advantage: the possibility of controlled photoluminescence for imaging applications and the capability to serve as a responsive nanoplatform for targeted NO delivery for advanced biomedical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: XPS analysis of CDs and Cd-FAR
ce:section.ce:para: The X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) examination provides detailed information regarding the elemental composition and surface chemistry of both the CDs and the functionalized CDs-FAR. Fig. 4 illustrates the XPS results for the CDs produced from chitosan and silk fibroin, emphasizing their structural and functional group makeup. Fig. 4(a) illustrates the XPS survey spectrum of CDs, highlighting the principal peaks for C1s, N1s, and O1s, so affirming the preeminent presence of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen. This composition highlights the successful production of nitrogen-doped carbon dots, presumably originating from chitosan [48]. Fig. 4(b) represents the high-resolution C1s spectrum, which is deconvoluted into three peaks: CC (∼284.5 eV), CN (∼285.6 eV), and CO (∼287.8 eV). These results validate the presence of aromatic and aliphatic carbons, nitrogen-functionalized carbon species, and oxidized functionalities, respectively, confirming partial oxidation and nitrogen doping inside the CD [38]. Fig. 4(c) displays the high-resolution N1s spectrum with peaks at ∼399.6 eV and ∼401.2 eV, corresponding to CN and NH bonds, indicating the incorporation of nitrogen functionalities from chitosan. Fig. 4(d) illustrates the high-resolution O1s spectrum, featuring peaks at ∼531.2 eV and ∼532.8 eV for CO and CO bonds [49], respectively, suggesting the oxidation of silk fibroin and chitosan, leading to oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface.

Moving to the functionalized carbon dots, Fig. 5 provides XPS characterization results for CDs-FAR, confirming the successful conjugation of Ru-NO complexes and FA to the CDs. Fig. 5(a) presents the survey spectrum, which prominently displays peaks for Ru3d and Ru3p along with C1s, N1s, and O1s peaks, validating the integration of ruthenium onto the CDs. The high-resolution spectrum of Ru3p in Fig. 5(b) shows distinct peaks at ∼463.5 eV and ∼485.9 eV, corresponding to the spin-orbit splitting of Ru3p₃/₂ and Ru3p₁/₂, respectively. This verifies the oxidation state of Ru in the functionalized CDs, signifying the existence of Ru-NO complexes. The incorporation of Ru and folic acid into CDs alters the elemental makeup and enhances the intricate surface chemistry of CDs-FAR, rendering them appropriate for applications necessitating NO release and targeted distribution via folate receptor recognition.

CDs have garnered significant attention in recent years due to their exceptional properties, including high surface area, superior biocompatibility, and adjustable fluorescence properties [50]. These attributes render them highly suitable for diverse applications, particularly as antioxidants. Antioxidants are compounds that neutralize detrimental free radicals and reactive oxygen species (ROS), thereby reducing oxidative stress linked to a range of diseases and the aging process [51].

To evaluate the antioxidant potential of CDs, two widely used assays—the DPPH and ABTS assays—are employed. These colorimetric methods measure the ability of antioxidants to reduce specific radicals, providing valuable insights into the free-radical scavenging potential of the material. The DPPH assay, a widely recognized and reliable technique for assessing antioxidant activity of materials. It utilizes DPPH, a stable nitrogen-centered free radical, characterized by its deep purple color. Upon interaction with an antioxidant, DPPH undergoes a reduction reaction, resulting in a noticeable color change from deep purple to yellow. This change is accompanied by a decrease in absorbance at 517 nm, correlating with the material's antioxidant capacity. For this study, CDs at varying concentrations (50–600 μg/mL) have been added to methanolic DPPH solutions, and the absorbance was recorded at 517 nm (not shown here). The % scavenging activity have calculated using Eq. (1) (Supporting Information), and represented in the graphs (details in Supporting Information). The results, depicted in Fig. 6a, demonstrate a dose-dependent increase in scavenging activity, ranging from 18 % at 50 μg/mL to 77 % at ∼450 μg/mL. The EC50 value, a critical parameter indicating the concentration required to achieve 50 % scavenging, was determined to be 198 μg/mL, underscoring the significant antioxidant capacity of CDs.

The ABTS assay further evaluated the antioxidant properties of CDs by measuring their ability to quench the blue-green ABTS+ radical cation, causing a reduction in absorbance at 734 nm. As shown in Fig. 6b, increasing concentrations of CDs (50–500 μg/mL) led to a significant reduction in ABTS+ absorbance, with scavenging activity increasing from 22 % to 89 %, with a visible color change from blue-green to colorless. The EC50 value for ABTS+ scavenging by CDs is determined to be 163 μg/mL, highlights the strong antioxidant potential of CDs. Extensive research has demonstrated that electron-donating functional groups on the surface of CDs, such as hydroxyl, carboxyl, and other reactive moieties, exhibit exceptional free radical scavenging capabilities [23]. Additionally, primary amino groups on the CDs have been reported to actively interact with free radicals, enhancing their antioxidant performance [52]. The presence of these functional groups on the CD surface is provided the CDs with a notable antioxidant capacity. Fig. 6c illustrates the underlying mechanism of action of CDs in radical scavenging, highlighting their utility in mitigating oxidative stress and their promise for biomedical applications. The antioxidant properties of CDs reduce reactive oxygen species (ROS), which could otherwise interact with NO and influence its bioavailability. By mitigating oxidative stress, CDs may indirectly assist in preserving the stability and regulated release of NO from CDs-FAR [53]. The Raman spectra of CDs before (Fig. S2) and after (Fig. S3) the ABTS experiment exhibited no significant changes in the D-band (1351 cm−1) and G-band (1548 cm−1) intensity ratio, indicating that the graphitic structure and defect density remained largely unaltered. This suggests that the CDs maintain their structural integrity during antioxidant interactions, highlighting their stability and potential for sustained radical scavenging activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Light-triggered NO release study of CDs-FAR
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 7 illustrates the release kinetics of nitric oxide (NO) from CDs-FAR nanoparticles under different light conditions in both aerobic and anaerobic saline solutions, demonstrating the nanoplatform's capacity for controlled NO delivery. Panels (a), (b), and (c) exhibit NO release profiles in an aerobic environment under light pulses at intensities of 100, 200, and 300 mW/cm2, respectively. Each peak in the profiles represents a unique light pulse, with each peak signifying a distinct release of NO. This trend indicates that higher light intensities enhance the excitation of the CDs-FAR nanoparticles, resulting in greater NO release per pulse. This intensity-dependent response highlights the photorelease mechanism of NO is engaged more effectively at elevated photon energy, facilitating a greater release of NO from the nanoparticle matrix. The NO quantum yield (Φ) was determined based on the NO concentration measured by a NO-sensitive electrode in aqueous solution, using a previously established technique [54]. We used blue (470 nm) and green (530 nm) light for excitation, yielding Φ values of 0.245 ± 0.02 and 0.148 ± 0.03 mol/einstein, respectively. The higher quantum yield under blue light compared to green light (530 nm) aligned with the electronic absorption characteristics of the nanoplatform in the visible spectrum.

At 100 mW/cm2 (Fig. 7(a)), each pulse induces a comparatively mild release of NO, with peak concentration values approximating 0.8 μM. When the intensity is increased to 200 mW/cm2 (Fig. 7(b)), the peak NO concentration rises to around 1.0 μM, indicating enhanced NO release per pulse. This trend persists at 300 mW/cm2 (Fig. 7(c)) with peak concentrations reach approximately 1.2 μM. The reduction in NO concentration between pulses results from the diffusion or reaction of the emitted NO in the aerobic saline milieu, where oxygen availability may accelerate NO breakdown. The progressive increase in peak NO release with light intensity underscores the sensitivity of CDs-FAR nanoparticles to photon fluxes, enabling precise modulation of NO delivery through controlled light parameters.

Fig. 7(d) provides a contrasting scenario, illustrating the release profile of NO under continuous light exposure at 300 mW/cm2 in an anaerobic saline solution. Unlike the pulsed light exposure in aerobic conditions, the NO concentration rises rapidly within the initial 10 min, reaching a stable plateau at approximately 8 μM. The absence of pulsed light exposure results in a prolonged release of NO, likely due to ongoing excitation and slow depletion of NO contained within the nanoparticle matrix. The plateau indicates that the rate of NO release ultimately aligns with the rate of NO depletion or saturation in the solution. The anaerobic conditions in Fig. 7(d) inhibit the quick breakdown of NO, facilitating its accumulation in the solution. This prolonged release pattern differs from the pulsed release observed in aerobic settings, where NO swiftly declines between pulses.

Overall, the data reveal that the release profile of NO from CDs-FAR nanoparticles can be finely tuned by adjusting light exposure modes (pulsed vs. continuous) and oxygen availability. Elevated light intensities and continuous illumination promote prolonged and elevated NO concentrations, demonstrating the system's potential for controlled therapeutic NO delivery under diverse environmental conditions. Our as-prepared nanoplatform is comparable with previously published studies on NO-releasing nanomaterials. Xue et al. demonstrated the use of nanodiamonds for NO delivery [29]; however, their system lacked the tunability and photoregulated control observed in our CDs-FAR-based platform. Similarly, studies on metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) and mesoporous silica nanoparticles (MSNs) have explored NO release, but they often require complex synthetic procedures and external co-factors for activation [55–57]. Compared to these systems, our CDs-FAR nanoplatform offers simpler synthesis, efficient light-triggered NO release, and inherent antioxidant properties, making it highly effective for biomedical applications. Light-triggered nitric oxide (NO) release has gained significant attention due to its potential in precise therapeutic applications. Various nanomaterial-based platforms have been developed to achieve controlled NO release upon light stimulation, including ruthenium-based complexes [58], metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) [59], mesoporous silica nanoparticles (MSNs) [60,61], and carbon-based nanomaterials [62,63]. Ruthenium nitrosyl complexes release NO upon light activation through photolysis; however, their clinical use is limited due to cytotoxicity and the requirement for UV light, which has limited tissue penetration. MOFs, composed of metal ions coordinated to organic ligands, offer tunable porosity for NO encapsulation and controlled release under light, enhancing photodynamic therapy (PDT), though concerns remain regarding their complex synthesis and long-term biocompatibility [64]. MSNs, known for their high surface area and tunable pore sizes, can be functionalized with photoresponsive molecules to release NO under light exposure, improving antibacterial activity, though slow release rates and stability issues exist. Carbon-based nanomaterials, particularly CDs, have emerged as promising candidates due to their excellent biocompatibility, tunable photoluminescence properties, and ability to release NO under visible light, providing a safer alternative to UV-responsive systems. Additionally, CDs can be functionalized for targeted delivery, enhancing therapeutic efficacy. Among these platforms, CDs offer a promising balance between efficient NO release, biocompatibility, and functionalization ease, making them advantageous for biomedical applications requiring precise NO delivery.

Fig. 8 illustrates the cytotoxic effects of CDs-FAR nanoparticles on HeLa cells under both dark and light-activated conditions, alongside the evaluation of singlet oxygen generation. Fig. 8(a) illustrates the viability of HeLa cells incubated for 6-hour with different concentrations of CDs-FAR nanoparticles (0–100 μg/mL) under two distinct conditions: dark (absence of light exposure) and light-exposed (10 min at 400 nm wavelength, 200 mW/cm2 intensity). The results demonstrate a concentration-dependent decline in cell viability under both conditions, with a more pronounced reduction observed in light-exposed samples, particularly at higher concentrations (80 and 100 μg/mL). This observation underscores the enhanced cytotoxicity induced by light activation, likely attributed to the photorelease of reactive species, such as nitric oxide (NO), from the CDs-FAR nanoparticles. The light-triggered toxicity mechanism suggests the potential utility of CDs-FAR for photodynamic therapy applications, wherein the controlled release of therapeutic agents can be achieved upon light activation.

Fig. 8(b) investigates the production of singlet oxygen (1O₂) by assessing the relative concentration of sodium 1,3-cyclohexadiene-1,4-diethanoate (CHDDE), a designated 1O₂ trap, throughout various irradiation durations (0–60 min). Both CDs and CDs-FAR nanoparticles were evaluated, revealing no substantial decrease in CHDDE concentration across all examined time interval (0–60 nm). This indicates that, under the experimental conditions, neither CDs nor CDs-FAR nanoparticles efficiently catalyze the synthesis of singlet oxygen, or that the created singlet oxygen is small and undetectable. This finding suggests that the observed cytotoxicity in Fig. 8(a) is likely attributable to the production of alternative species such as NO, rather than singlet oxygen, with the light-induced response predominantly influencing cell viability in the context of CDs-FAR nanoparticles.

The controlled NO release from CDs-FAR ensures a gradual and effective induction of apoptosis, avoiding excessive cytotoxicity that could affect healthy cells. Moreover, the difference between light and dark conditions demonstrates the specificity and controllability of the NO release mechanism. The obtained results confirm the efficient intracellular delivery and therapeutic potential of our system. The observed decrease in HeLa cell viability under light stimulation aligns with the expected therapeutic effect of controlled NO release. The difference between light and dark conditions confirmed the photo-responsive behavior of CDs-FAR, ensuring precise NO delivery. This controlled release mechanism prevents excessive toxicity, maintaining a balance between therapeutic efficiency and safety [65].

This work presents the successful synthesis and surface modification of antioxidant carbon dots (CDs) by functionalizing naturally derived CDs with folic acid (FA) and a ruthenium-nitrosyl (Ru-NO) complex. Comprehensive characterization using XPS, PL spectroscopy, UV–visible spectroscopy, XRD, and FTIR confirmed the effective integration of FA and Ru-NO onto the CDs, providing valuable structural and functional insights. The excitation-dependent fluorescence observed in PL experiments highlights their potential for multifunctional optical applications, while antioxidant assays validated their intrinsic antioxidant activity, attributed to nitrogen and oxygen-rich surface functionalities. A key highlight of this study is the light-activated NO release mechanism of CDs-FAR, enabling precise and controlled NO delivery upon visible light irradiation. This light-activated activity is essential for therapeutic applications, especially in the regulation of physiological processes where precise NO release is crucial. Cytotoxicity studies revealed exceptional biocompatibility of CDs-FAR in dark conditions, with enhanced therapeutic efficacy under visible light exposure without considerable toxicity, showcasing their potential for targeted, light-mediated biological applications. The surface-engineered CDs-FAR effectively combine the biocompatibility and fluorescence properties of naturally sourced CDs, the selective targeting ability of folic acid, and the therapeutic advantages of light-triggered NO release. These integrated features make CDs-FAR a promising platform for advancing NO-based therapies, particularly in applications where oxidative stress modulation, targeted drug delivery, and precise therapeutic control are imperative. Future studies could explore their potential in managing diseases where oxidative stress and localized nitric oxide delivery play pivotal roles, paving the way for next-generation NO-delivery systems in biomedical science.
